Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place, which I'm not proud of. I tried to dedicate about 30 hours a week, usually in chunks when I could find time between everything else going on in my life. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes from the materials. But with limited resources at home, I often had to plan around when I could access reliable internet or find a quiet space to focus.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was demanding my attention. I'm 28, so I have responsibilities outside of school that younger students might not have. Some weeks I could stick to a schedule, but other weeks life just got in the way and I'd have to cram everything into a few intense days. The stress of that inconsistency really wore me down over time.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there was something new posted or realizing a deadline was approaching. I'd get a notification or suddenly remember I needed to submit something, and that panic would push me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch the lecture videos because those were the most helpful for how I learn. It wasn't always planned—more reactive than proactive, if I'm being honest.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester where logging in became more routine for me. I started checking in most days of the week, especially to watch videos and see discussion posts. It happened because I found the video content genuinely helpful and I wanted to keep up with what others were saying. But when stress got really high, that routine fell apart and it became harder to maintain.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner. Seeing concepts explained with diagrams and demonstrations made things click in a way that reading text alone never did. I also found some of the peer discussions helpful—not always for the content, but for realizing other people were struggling with the same things I was. That felt validating somehow.
Q6: I often ignored or skimmed through the reading materials and some of the optional resources. Honestly, with my low resources situation, I had to prioritize what would give me the most benefit for my time. Long text documents just didn't stick with me the way visual content did. I also delayed discussion posts sometimes because formulating responses felt draining when I was already stressed.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to what I needed to understand or submit. If it connected directly to an assignment or exam topic, I was more likely to engage deeply. Visual content that broke down complex ideas also felt valuable because I could actually retain that information. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade felt harder to justify.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd cut when time got tight. Required activities had this weight of consequences attached to them, so I'd push through even when I was exhausted. Optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, even when part of me knew they might help. There was always this calculation of effort versus immediate payoff happening in my head.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded lecture slides and videos when I could and studied them offline constantly. I'd print things out when possible and annotate them by hand because that helped me remember better. I also used some external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't fully understand from the course materials alone. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying from my printed notes or downloaded materials for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd review my handwritten diagrams and summaries repeatedly before exams—that was real studying, but it left no digital trace. Sometimes I'd re-watch downloaded videos multiple times offline. The LMS only captured the initial download, not the hours of review that followed.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy. I'd access a video or document once, download it if possible, and then study it repeatedly offline. Internet access isn't always reliable for me, so having materials saved locally was essential. I'd watch the same lecture video three or four times from my downloads while the LMS only showed one view. It was a survival strategy given my limited resources.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became really difficult. When my stress was at its peak, I'd feel paralyzed and unable to focus on anything. Around midterms, I remember feeling completely overwhelmed and my attendance dropped because I just couldn't face logging in. Life circumstances outside of school also piled up, and I don't have much resilience to bounce back quickly from setbacks.
Q13: All of those things affected me significantly. Fatigue was constant because I was pushing myself so hard with 30 hours of study while managing everything else. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections of the LMS entirely sometimes. When competing responsibilities demanded attention, the LMS was often what got sacrificed first because the consequences weren't as immediate as other obligations.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too tired or anxious to actually absorb anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something productive. Those sessions felt hollow, and I knew I'd have to revisit everything later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger or more complex the task, the more I'd avoid starting it. I'd tell myself I needed to understand the material better first, but really I was just scared of doing poorly. My low motivation made it hard to push through that avoidance, and then deadlines would sneak up on me.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before something was due, logging in multiple times to check requirements and submit work. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less consistently. It's not the healthiest pattern, but the pressure of consequences was often the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and high stress.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When it was encouraging, it gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback sometimes made me want to withdraw because my resilience is pretty low. After the CBT session I had, I tried to reframe how I received feedback, but it's still a struggle. I'd sometimes delay checking my grades because I was anxious about what I'd see.
Q18: The course structure influenced my participation quite a bit. When things were clearly organized and I knew what was expected, I could plan better. But when requirements were vague or spread across different sections of the LMS, I'd get confused and frustrated. The weekly rhythm helped somewhat, but I needed more explicit guidance than what was provided to feel confident about what to prioritize.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched my learning style perfectly. Having discussion forums also helped me feel less isolated in my struggles. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy resources without visual aids—I'd lose focus quickly. Also, the platform itself wasn't always intuitive, and navigating it added unnecessary friction when I was already stressed.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about what they're supposed to do. They might be searching through materials repeatedly, trying to understand requirements, without actually producing anything. It could also be someone like me who engages a lot with content but struggles to complete assignments due to stress or competing demands. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually does reflect part of my experience. Some of my best studying happened offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. If someone is efficient with their time or learns well independently, they might not need to be constantly logged into the LMS. I think I performed reasonably well on exams because of all that invisible offline work, even though my assignment completion was lower than I wanted.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns of when people are active and what they click on. They probably capture deadline-driven behavior pretty accurately. But they miss all the offline studying, the quality of engagement versus just clicking through, and the emotional state behind the activity. They can't tell if someone watched a video while actually learning or while completely zoned out from exhaustion.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to dig deeper, when I'm making connections between concepts. For me, it's also about feeling mentally present and capable of absorbing information. Unfortunately, my high stress and low motivation made true engagement feel rare this semester, even when I was technically doing the work.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled emotionally throughout this course, or how the CBT session helped me cope better toward the end. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes spread across my kitchen table, or the anxiety attacks before submitting assignments. It also wouldn't show that despite everything—the stress, the limited resources, the low motivation—I genuinely tried my best and surprised myself by earning a Merit. That resilience, however small, doesn't show up in any log.
